---
title: "Ch 7 ‚Äî Similarity"
subtitle: "Same shape, any size ‚Äî and Pythagoras falls out as a corollary"
lesson-id: "kg1.similarity"
tier: "kg1"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book I (Planimetry)"
kiselev-chapter: 7
kiselev-chapter-title: "Similarity"
kiselev-sections: "Planimetry ¬ß¬ß156‚Äì185 (Thales' intercepts, similar triangles, Pythagoras)"
prerequisites: [kg1.parallels, kg1.triangle-congruence]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, ai-ml, research]
estimated-minutes: 55
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the *alternate-interior-angles* theorem? See
   [`kg1.parallels`](../04-parallels/index.qmd).
2. Are SSS / SAS / ASA reflexive for you? See
   [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd).
3. Can you handle ratios and proportions: $\dfrac{a}{b} =
   \dfrac{c}{d} \Leftrightarrow ad = bc$?
4. Do you know that the *area of a triangle* is $\tfrac{1}{2}
   \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

Two triangles, side by side. Drag a slider ‚Äî one of them scales
up. The angles never change; only the sizes.

```{ojs}
viewof scale = Inputs.range([0.5, 2.5], {value: 1.5, step: 0.1, label: "Scale factor k"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof shapeAngle = Inputs.range([20, 80], {value: 50, step: 1, label: "Apex angle (¬∞)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 480, h = 280, padX = 30, padY = 30;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 12]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 5]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  const rad = d => d * Math.PI / 180;
  const drawTri = (ox, base, lab) => {
    const apexAng = rad(shapeAngle);
    // place A at (ox, 0), B at (ox+base, 0), C above forming apex angle at A
    const A = [ox, 0];
    const B = [ox + base, 0];
    const C = [ox + base * 0.6 * Math.cos(apexAng) + base * 0.05,
               base * 0.7 * Math.sin(apexAng) + 0.6];
    svg.append("polygon")
      .attr("points", `${xS(A[0])},${yS(A[1])} ${xS(B[0])},${yS(B[1])} ${xS(C[0])},${yS(C[1])}`)
      .attr("fill", "#26a").attr("fill-opacity", 0.18)
      .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);
    const labels = ["A","B","C"];
    [A, B, C].forEach((p, i) => {
      svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(p[0]) + 4).attr("y", yS(p[1]) - 4)
        .attr("font-size", 11).text(`${labels[i]}${lab}`);
    });
  };

  drawTri(0, 2, "");          // small one
  drawTri(5, 2 * scale, "'"); // scaled one

  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
    .attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`‚ñ≥ABC and ‚ñ≥A'B'C' are similar with ratio k = ${scale.toFixed(2)}.`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY + 6)
    .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`Same angles (apex = ${shapeAngle}¬∞). All sides scaled by k.`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The two triangles have **the same angles** and **proportional sides**.
That is what *similar* means.

## Wonder

1. Two triangles have the same three angles. Must they be similar?
   *(Try to imagine a counterexample.)*
2. Two triangles have proportional sides $a' = ka, b' = kb, c' = kc$.
   Must they be similar?
3. A triangle and a *scaled copy* ‚Äî what is the ratio of their
   *areas* (not sides)?
4. Pythagoras: in a right triangle, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. Is this
   somehow a *similarity* statement in disguise?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) **Yes** ‚Äî the *AA* similarity criterion (any two equal angles
force the third by the angle-sum theorem). (2) **Yes** ‚Äî the *SSS*
similarity criterion. (3) **$k^2$** ‚Äî areas scale by the *square*
of the linear ratio. (4) Yes ‚Äî the cleanest proof of Pythagoras
drops the altitude from the right angle and uses two pairs of
similar triangles.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 ‚Äî The intercept theorem (Thales)

Three parallel lines $\ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3$ cut two transversals.
The transversals meet $\ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3$ at $A_1, A_2, A_3$
and $B_1, B_2, B_3$ respectively. Show that
$$
\dfrac{|A_1 A_2|}{|A_2 A_3|} = \dfrac{|B_1 B_2|}{|B_2 B_3|}.
$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
The classical proof: when the ratio is *rational*, divide both sides
into equal sub-pieces using parallels through the divisions. For
*irrational* ratios use the standard Eudoxean / continuity argument.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Rational case.* If $|A_1 A_2| : |A_2 A_3| = p : q$ in lowest terms,
divide $A_1 A_3$ into $p + q$ equal parts and through each division
point draw a parallel to $\ell_1$. By construction these parallels
cut $A_1 A_3$ into $p + q$ equal pieces; by alternate-interior + ASA
they also cut the second transversal $B_1 B_3$ into $p + q$ equal
pieces. So $|B_1 B_2| : |B_2 B_3| = p : q$ as well.

*Irrational case.* Take rational approximations from above and below;
both ratios are pinched. *(Kiselev gives the rational case in ¬ß158
and refers the irrational to the *theory of magnitudes* in an
appendix; for school it is taken as a continuity statement.)*
**QED. ‚Äî *Thales' intercept theorem.* Planimetry ¬ß158.**
</details>

### Problem 2 ‚Äî A line parallel to a side cuts the other two
sides proportionally

In $\triangle ABC$, a line parallel to side $BC$ meets $AB$ at $D$
and $AC$ at $E$. Show that $\dfrac{|AD|}{|DB|} = \dfrac{|AE|}{|EC|}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Take $\ell_1 = $ line $DE$, $\ell_2 = $ line $BC$, and a third
parallel through $A$. Apply Thales' intercept theorem with
transversals $AB$ and $AC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The line through $A$ parallel to $BC$, the line $DE \parallel BC$,
and the line $BC$ itself are three parallels. The transversal $AB$
cuts them at $A, D, B$; the transversal $AC$ at $A, E, C$. By
Thales (Problem 1):
$\dfrac{|AD|}{|DB|} = \dfrac{|AE|}{|EC|}$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß159 ‚Äî the *basic proportionality theorem*,
or *BPT*.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 ‚Äî AA similarity criterion

Two triangles have two pairs of equal angles. Show they are similar.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
The third angles are also equal (angle-sum). Lay one triangle on
the other matching the equal angles; the third sides are parallel.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle A'B'C'$ have $\angle A = \angle
A'$ and $\angle B = \angle B'$. Then $\angle C = \angle C'$
(angle-sum). Place $A' \to A$ and rotate so $A'B'$ lies along $AB$;
the side $A'B'$ extends to some length along $AB$, ending at a
point $B''$ with $|AB''| = |A'B'|$. The angle at $A$ matches; the
angle at $B''$ matches $\angle B' = \angle B$. So line $B''C''$ is
parallel to $BC$ (corresponding angles). By Problem 2:
$\dfrac{|AB''|}{|B''B|} + 1 = \dfrac{|AB|}{|AB''|}$, etc. ‚Äî the
ratios of corresponding sides are all equal. **Triangles similar.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß161.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 ‚Äî SSS similarity

Two triangles have all three pairs of sides proportional. Show they
are similar.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Construct a triangle similar to the first by AA, then show it is
*congruent* to the second by SSS.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Suppose $a' / a = b' / b = c' / c = k$ for triangles with sides
$a, b, c$ and $a', b', c'$. Construct $\triangle A'' B'' C''$ inside
$\triangle ABC$ as the unique triangle similar to $\triangle ABC$
with linear scale $k$ ‚Äî by Problem 2 (cut a line parallel to one
side), this triangle exists and has sides $ka, kb, kc = a', b', c'$.
By SSS congruence, $\triangle A''B''C'' \cong \triangle A'B'C'$.
Both are similar to $\triangle ABC$ ‚Äî hence $\triangle A'B'C' \sim
\triangle ABC$. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß162.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 ‚Äî Area scales with the square

Two similar triangles have linear scale ratio $k$. Show their areas
have ratio $k^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Triangle area $= \tfrac{1}{2} (\text{base}) (\text{height})$. Both
base and height scale by $k$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
If the linear scale is $k$, then base is $k \cdot b$ and height is
$k \cdot h$. Area: $\tfrac{1}{2} (kb)(kh) = k^2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}
bh = k^2 \cdot \text{(original area)}$. **Ratio of areas: $k^2$.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß164. ‚Äî generalises to all similar figures, not just
triangles.)*
</details>

### Problem 6 ‚Äî The Pythagorean theorem via similarity

In right triangle $ABC$ with right angle at $C$, drop the altitude
$CH$ to hypotenuse $AB$. Show $|AC|^2 + |BC|^2 = |AB|^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
The altitude $CH$ creates **three similar triangles**: the original
$\triangle ABC$, $\triangle ACH$, and $\triangle CBH$. Express
$|AH|$ and $|BH|$ in terms of the sides via the similarities, then
add.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $a = |BC|, b = |AC|, c = |AB|, h = |CH|, p = |AH|, q = |BH|$
(so $p + q = c$).

$\triangle ABC \sim \triangle ACH$ (both have $\angle A$ and a right
angle). Ratio of corresponding sides: $\dfrac{c}{b} = \dfrac{b}{p}
$, hence $b^2 = cp$.

$\triangle ABC \sim \triangle CBH$ (both have $\angle B$ and a right
angle). Ratio: $\dfrac{c}{a} = \dfrac{a}{q}$, hence $a^2 = cq$.

Adding: $a^2 + b^2 = c(p + q) = c \cdot c = c^2$. **QED.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß170 ‚Äî the cleanest proof of Pythagoras in school
geometry.)*
</details>

### Problem 7 ‚Äî The angle bisector divides proportionally

In $\triangle ABC$, the bisector of $\angle A$ meets $BC$ at $D$.
Show $\dfrac{|BD|}{|DC|} = \dfrac{|AB|}{|AC|}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Through $C$ draw a line parallel to $AB$, meeting line $AD$ at $E$.
Compare $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ECD$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Through $C$, draw $\ell \parallel AB$, meeting $AD$ extended at $E$.
$\angle ABD = \angle DEC$ (alternate-interior, $AB \parallel \ell$).
$\angle ADB = \angle EDC$ (vertical). So $\triangle ABD \sim
\triangle ECD$ (AA). Hence $\dfrac{|BD|}{|DC|} = \dfrac{|AB|}{|EC|}$.
Now $\angle BAE = \angle DAC$ (bisector), and $\angle BAE =
\angle CEA$ (alternate-interior), so $\angle CEA = \angle DAC$ ‚Äî
i.e. $\triangle EAC$ is isosceles with $|AC| = |EC|$. Substitute:
$\dfrac{|BD|}{|DC|} = \dfrac{|AB|}{|AC|}$. **QED.** *(Planimetry
¬ß175 ‚Äî the *angle-bisector theorem*.)*
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definitions.**

- **Similar polygons** (denoted $\sim$): same number of sides; equal
  corresponding angles; corresponding sides in a common ratio (the
  *scale factor* $k$).

**Similarity criteria for triangles** *(equivalent ‚Äî only need one)*:

1. **AA** ‚Äî two pairs of equal angles ‚áí similar.
2. **SAS-similarity** ‚Äî one equal angle and proportional sides about
   it ‚áí similar.
3. **SSS-similarity** ‚Äî three pairs of proportional sides ‚áí similar.

**Theorems (Planimetry ¬ß¬ß156‚Äì185).**

- *Thales' intercept theorem:* parallel lines cut transversals into
  proportional segments.
- *Basic proportionality:* a line parallel to one side of a triangle
  cuts the other two proportionally (and conversely).
- *Areas of similar figures:* in ratio $k^2$ where $k$ is the
  linear ratio.
- *Pythagoras:* $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ in a right triangle (proved via
  similar triangles formed by the altitude to the hypotenuse).
- *Angle-bisector theorem:* the bisector of $\angle A$ divides the
  opposite side in the ratio of the adjacent sides.

:::

A verse:

> *Same angles, sides in fixed ratio ‚Äî that's similarity, full stop.*
> *AA, SSS, SAS ‚Äî three doors, one room, take whichever you've got.*
> *Length scales by k, area by k-squared, volume by k-cubed (next book).*
> *Drop the altitude in a right triangle ‚Äî Pythagoras falls out, no extra hook.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A vertical pole of height $5$ m casts a shadow of length $3$ m on
> level ground. At the same moment, a tree casts a shadow of $9$ m.
> How tall is the tree?

The pole-and-shadow and the tree-and-shadow form similar right
triangles (sun's rays are parallel, ground is shared). Ratios:
$\dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}}$ is constant.

$\dfrac{5}{3} = \dfrac{H}{9}$, so $H = \dfrac{5 \cdot 9}{3} = 15$.

**Tree is $15$ m tall.** *(Thales reportedly used exactly this
trick to measure the Great Pyramid.)*

### Practice ‚Äî Warm

**W1.** $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ with $|AB| = 6, |DE| =
9$. The scale factor?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$k = 9/6 = 3/2$.
</details>

**W2.** Two similar triangles have areas $25$ and $100$. The ratio
of their corresponding sides?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$k^2 = 100/25 = 4$, so $k = 2$.
</details>

**W3.** A right triangle has legs $5$ and $12$. Find the
hypotenuse.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Working

**P1.** In $\triangle ABC$, $D$ is on $AB$ and $E$ on $AC$ with $DE
\parallel BC$. If $|AD| = 4, |DB| = 6, |AC| = 15$, find $|AE|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
By BPT: $|AD|/|DB| = |AE|/|EC|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{|AE|}{15 - |AE|}$. Cross-multiply:
$60 - 4|AE| = 6|AE|$, so $|AE| = 6$.
</details>

**P2.** A right triangle has hypotenuse $25$ and one leg $7$. Find
the other leg, and the altitude from the right-angle vertex to the
hypotenuse.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Other leg: $\sqrt{625 - 49} = \sqrt{576} = 24$. Altitude
$h = (\text{legs product}) / \text{hypotenuse} = 7 \cdot 24 / 25 =
6.72$.
</details>

**P3.** In $\triangle ABC$, the bisector of $\angle A$ meets $BC$
at $D$. If $|AB| = 8, |AC| = 6, |BC| = 7$, find $|BD|$ and $|DC|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
$|BD|/|DC| = |AB|/|AC| = 8/6 = 4/3$. And $|BD| + |DC| = 7$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Set $|BD| = 4t, |DC| = 3t$. Then $7t = 7$, so $t = 1$. $|BD| = 4,
|DC| = 3$.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Stretch

**S1.** Show that in any triangle, the three medians **meet at one
point** which divides each median in ratio $2 : 1$ from vertex to
midpoint. *(The centroid.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Let medians from $A$ and $B$ meet at $G$. Use BPT or similar
triangles to show $|AG| : |GD| = 2 : 1$ where $D$ is the midpoint
of $BC$. Then show the median from $C$ also passes through $G$ at
the same ratio.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $D$ = midpoint of $BC$, $E$ = midpoint of $AC$, $G$ = $AD \cap
BE$. Connect midpoints ‚Äî $DE \parallel AB$ (midsegment) with $|DE|
= \tfrac{1}{2}|AB|$. So $\triangle ABG \sim \triangle DEG$ with
ratio $2 : 1$ (AA: vertical angles at $G$, alternate-interior at
$AB \parallel DE$). Hence $|AG| : |GD| = 2 : 1$, and similarly
$|BG| : |GE| = 2 : 1$. The same applies to the third median (by
identical argument), so it passes through the same $G$. **Three
medians concur at $G$ ‚Äî the *centroid* ‚Äî at $2:1$.** *(Planimetry
¬ß168.)*
</details>

**S2.** Stewart's theorem: in $\triangle ABC$ with cevian $AD$
(from $A$ to a point $D$ on $BC$ with $|BD| = m, |DC| = n,
|BC| = m + n$), and $|AD| = d$, then
$$b^2 m + c^2 n - a m n = d^2 (m + n).$$
*(Where $a = |BC|, b = |AC|, c = |AB|$.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Apply the law of cosines in $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ACD$
using the same $\cos \angle ADB = -\cos \angle ADC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\theta = \angle ADB$, so $\angle ADC = 180¬∞ - \theta$ and
$\cos \angle ADC = -\cos\theta$. In $\triangle ABD$: $c^2 = m^2 +
d^2 - 2md\cos\theta$. In $\triangle ACD$: $b^2 = n^2 + d^2 +
2nd\cos\theta$. Multiply the first by $n$, the second by $m$, add:
$c^2 n + b^2 m = (m^2 n + n^2 m) + d^2 (m + n) - 2mnd\cos\theta +
2mnd\cos\theta = mn(m + n) + d^2(m + n)$. Rearrange to
$b^2 m + c^2 n - amn = d^2(m + n)$, with $a = m + n$. **QED.**
*(A staple stretch problem; Kiselev addresses it implicitly via
the Apollonius theorem in ¬ß181.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Planimetry*, ¬ß¬ß158‚Äì185.

**K1.** In $\triangle ABC$, the altitude from $C$ to $AB$ has foot
$H$. Show $|CH|^2 = |AH| \cdot |HB|$ (the *geometric-mean
relation*).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
$\triangle AHC \sim \triangle CHB$ ‚Äî both have a right angle and
share the angle at $C$ (well, complementary angles).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle ACB$ has right angle at $C$ (assumption: $C$ is the
right-angle vertex; otherwise the statement holds only when the
altitude lands inside the triangle and we adjust). Both
$\triangle AHC$ and $\triangle CHB$ are right at $H$ and share
$\angle CAH = \angle BCH$ (both complement to $\angle ACH$ ‚Äî yes,
this is the classic mutual-complement argument). By **AA** they are
similar. So $\dfrac{|AH|}{|CH|} = \dfrac{|CH|}{|HB|}$, i.e.
$|CH|^2 = |AH| \cdot |HB|$. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß171.)*
</details>

**K2.** A point $P$ inside $\triangle ABC$ is connected to the three
vertices, dividing the triangle into three sub-triangles with areas
$S_a, S_b, S_c$ (opposite to $A, B, C$). Show that the cevians from
$A, B, C$ through $P$ divide the *opposite sides* in ratios
$|BD|/|DC| = S_b / S_c$, etc., where $D = AP \cap BC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Triangles $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ACD$ share the altitude
from $A$. Likewise $\triangle PBD$ and $\triangle PCD$ share the
altitude from $P$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ACD$ share the altitude from $A$, so
their areas are in ratio $|BD| : |DC|$. Same for $\triangle PBD$
and $\triangle PCD$. Subtracting (since $\triangle ABP =
\triangle ABD - \triangle PBD$, etc.): the ratios still equal
$|BD| : |DC|$. So $S_c / S_b = |BD| / |DC|$ ‚Äî wait, I need to track
the labelling: the area of $\triangle BPC$ uses base $BC$ and is
across from $A$, hence $S_a$. By the cevian-area splitting:
$|BD|/|DC| = $ (area of $\triangle ABP$ + $\triangle BPD$ piece...)
The clean statement: **the cevian from $A$ through $P$ hits $BC$ at
the point dividing $BC$ in ratio $S_c : S_b$.** *(Generalises Ceva's
theorem; Kiselev's ¬ß177.)*
</details>

**K3.** A circle of radius $r$ is inscribed in $\triangle ABC$
(touches each side internally). Show that
$$\text{Area}(\triangle ABC) = r \cdot s$$
where $s = (a + b + c) / 2$ is the *semi-perimeter*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Connect the incentre $I$ to each vertex, splitting the triangle
into three sub-triangles whose altitudes from $I$ are all $r$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Connect the incentre $I$ to each vertex. The three sub-triangles
$\triangle IBC, \triangle ICA, \triangle IAB$ have bases $a, b, c$
respectively and altitudes all equal to $r$ (the inradius ‚Äî distance
from $I$ to each side, equal because $I$ is on each angle bisector
and the perpendicular distances from $I$ to the sides are equal).
Sum of areas:
$\tfrac{1}{2}(ar + br + cr) = \tfrac{1}{2} r (a + b + c) = rs$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß180 ‚Äî the *triangle-inradius identity*.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book I ‚Äî Planimetry*, ¬ß¬ß158‚Äì185 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
K1's geometric-mean relation is Kiselev ¬ß171 verbatim in shape;
K3's inradius identity is ¬ß180.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Similar means same area."** It does not ‚Äî *similar* allows
   any scale factor $k > 0$. Same area is a special case of similar
   (with $k = 1$, i.e. congruent), but most similar triangles have
   *different* sizes.
2. **"AA only works if you have all three equal angles."** Two are
   enough ‚Äî the third is forced by the angle-sum theorem. Hence the
   criterion is *AA*, not *AAA*.
3. **"Pythagoras is its own theorem; similarity is a separate
   topic."** They are deeply linked. Kiselev's own proof of
   Pythagoras (Problem 6) is by similarity ‚Äî the altitude-to-
   hypotenuse construction creates two triangles each similar to
   the whole, and Pythagoras is the algebra.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you prove Pythagoras (Problem 6) on a fresh sheet, drawing
   the altitude and stating the two similarities?
2. Did the area-ratio result (Problem 5) feel like a natural
   consequence of "scale base √ó scale height"?
3. Can you explain why **AA** is enough ‚Äî and why two equal angles
   force the third ‚Äî without consulting notes?

- **3 yes:** move on to *Regular Polygons* (Ch 8).
- **2 yes:** redo Problems 2 and 6 ‚Äî BPT and Pythagoras are the
  chapter's twin pillars.
- **0‚Äì1 yes:** Problems 1 ‚Üí 2 ‚Üí 3 form a chain; replay them in
  order.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg1.parallels`](../04-parallels/index.qmd) ‚Äî alternate-interior
  angles + the parallel postulate, used throughout BPT and AA.
- [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  SSS as the comparator for the SSS-similarity proof.

### What this unlocks

- **`kg1.regular-polygons`** *(Ch 8, planned)* ‚Äî every regular
  $n$-gon is similar to every other regular $n$-gon.
- **`kg1.areas`** *(Ch 9, planned)* ‚Äî Heron's formula via the
  inradius identity (K3).
- [`kg2.cylinder-cone`](../../tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/03-cylinder-cone/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  similar cones (linear ratio $k$ ‚áí area ratio $k^2$, volume ratio
  $k^3$).
- [`t3.trig-ratios`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/07-trig-ratios/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  trigonometric ratios are *similar-triangle ratios* ‚Äî they depend
  on the angle, not on the size of the triangle.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book I ‚Äî Chapter 7: Similarity (¬ß¬ß156‚Äì185).**
  Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t2.similarity-scale`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)
  (the *Touch* idiom of similarity);
  [`cbse10.triangles-similarity`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/06-triangles-similarity/index.qmd)
  (board-exam-style similarity proofs);
  [`t2.pythagorean-theorem`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)
  (alternative proofs of Pythagoras).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development
  of the Eudoxean theory of magnitudes underlying the irrational
  case of Thales (¬ß158 appendix).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every camera projection ‚Äî orthographic,
perspective, or oblique ‚Äî is a *similarity transformation* of some
projected plane. The fact that perspective preserves angles
(approximately, for narrow fields) is exactly why a 3D scene
remains *recognisable* when projected to a flat screen.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** **Convolutional neural networks** are built on a
similarity assumption ‚Äî that local patterns in an image are
*scale-invariant*: a cat detector should fire whether the cat is
near or far. The pyramid / multi-scale architectures used in modern
vision encode this assumption as similar-triangle reasoning at the
feature level.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** *Fractal geometry* studies sets that are exactly
similar to themselves at every scale (the Mandelbrot set, the Koch
snowflake). The dimension of a fractal is fundamentally a
*similarity-scaling* exponent.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the ¬ß¬ß158‚Äì185 ordering); Eudoxus (the
  irrational-ratio handling, deferred); Thales (the
  shadow-comparison Worked Example).
- **The Pythagoras-via-similarity proof (Problem 6)** is the one I
  recommend memorising ‚Äî it generalises directly to non-Euclidean
  geometries (where it *fails*, telling you the geometry is curved)
  and is the fastest route to the law of cosines.
- **What this lesson does not do:** prove the *concurrence of
  angle bisectors* (the incentre exists), only *uses* it in K3.
  The concurrence proof belongs in Ch 8 or as a direct corollary of
  the locus theorem from Ch 3.
:::
